ontology.foodex2_description: Pumpkin / butternut squash (Cucurbita spp.), raw, peeled and deseeded

main_ingredients: pumpkin / butternut squash (Cucurbita moschata or Cucurbita maxima), raw peeled flesh

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard cooking portion of pumpkin is approximately 120g cubed raw — 1 cup or 1 thick wedge purchased from a Singapore wet market.

nutrient_source.title: Pumpkin (raw, peeled) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14040053; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F14040053 — Pumpkin, raw, peeled and deseeded.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled pumpkin (approximately 75). Raw pumpkin GI not applicable. Despite the high GI, the very low carbohydrate content (6.5g/100g) results in a low glycaemic load per serving (~5.9 for 120g). Pumpkin is a 'high GI, low glycaemic load' food.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (1mg/100g) — one of the lowest of any vegetable. High potassium (340mg/100g). Very low calorie. Pumpkin soup is one of the most naturally low-sodium comfort foods.
hypertension.guidance: Pumpkin is highly recommended for patients with hypertension. Plain pumpkin soup (blended pumpkin with minimal salt) is an ideal meal component — naturally sweet and creamy without added salt. Avoid canned pumpkin soups which are high in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: While cooked pumpkin has a high GI (~75), the actual glycaemic load per serving (120g) is only ~5.9 due to very low digestible carbohydrate content. Pumpkin is a 'high GI, low GL' food — a commonly misunderstood example for diabetes education.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should not avoid pumpkin based on its GI alone. The low glycaemic load per serving means it causes minimal blood glucose elevation at realistic portions. Pumpkin is far preferable to white rice as a carbohydrate component. Include as a free vegetable in meal planning.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Good beta-carotene/vitamin A (426mcg RAE/100g) and vitamin E (1.1mg/100g) — both antioxidant carotenoids. Very low calorie dense, allowing generous consumption.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pumpkin is a beneficial vegetable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Its antioxidant carotenoids (beta-carotene, lutein, zeaxanthin) support anti-inflammatory activity. Include generously in soups and stir-fries.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pumpkin (Cucurbita moschata or Cucurbita maxima) is a yellow-orange cucurbit vegetable widely consumed in Singapore wet markets (sold pre-cut in wedges). Despite having a high GI (~75) when cooked, the actual glycaemic load per normal serving is very low because pumpkin has very little digestible starch (6.5g carbohydrate/100g, mostly from water). An important teaching example for separating GI from GL. Very high in beta-carotene (426mcg RAE/100g), naturally sweet, extremely low in sodium (1mg/100g), and low calorie (26 kcal/100g). Used in Singapore in pumpkin soup, stir-fried pumpkin, pumpkin congee, curry, and as a side vegetable in Chinese family cooking.

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F14040053).
notes: Values per 100g raw peeled deseeded pumpkin flesh. The important clinical teaching point: pumpkin has a high GI (~75) but a very LOW glycaemic load (~5.9 per 120g serving) because it has very little carbohydrate per 100g. Patients with diabetes should be reassured about pumpkin. The seeds (pepitas) have entirely different nutritional values — this record is for flesh only.
